basic_info.name: Cocrystal Screening
basic_info.name_ja: 共結晶スクリーニング
basic_info.description: Cocrystals are multi-component crystalline forms where an API and a coformer are held together by non-covalent interactions, typically hydrogen bonds. HSP distance between API and coformer predicts the likelihood of cocrystal formation: components with similar HSP values (small Ra) are more likely to form stable cocrystals. This enables rapid virtual screening of coformer libraries before labor-intensive experimental crystallization trials, significantly reducing the number of experiments needed for polymorph and cocrystal exploration.

hsp_principle.hsp_principle: Cocrystal formation requires sufficient molecular complementarity for heteromeric interactions (API-coformer) to be energetically competitive with homomeric interactions (API-API and coformer-coformer). HSP proximity in Hansen space indicates similar intermolecular interaction profiles, which is a necessary (though not sufficient) condition for cocrystal formation. The hydrogen bonding component dH is particularly critical because most pharmaceutical cocrystals are sustained by hydrogen bonding synthons. The approach uses Ra distance as a pre-screening filter to prioritize coformer candidates.
hsp_principle.typical_red_threshold: [uncertain] Ra < 7 MPa^0.5 suggested for cocrystal formation likelihood; some studies use component-specific thresholds

required_inputs: Hydrogen bonding donor/acceptor profiles of API and coformer
Molecular structure information for synthon analysis
data_sources: Group contribution methods (Hoftyzer-Van Krevelen) for HSP estimation
HSPiP database for common coformers (carboxylic acids, amides, etc.)
Cambridge Structural Database (CSD) for known cocrystal structures
Literature compilations of pharmaceutical coformer HSP values
complementary_parameters: pKa values (for salt vs cocrystal determination, delta-pKa rule)

limitations: HSP distance is a thermodynamic approximation that does not capture the directional and geometric specificity of hydrogen bonding synthons critical for cocrystal formation
Cannot distinguish between cocrystal, salt, eutectic, and solid solution outcomes
Polymorphism of the cocrystal itself is not predicted
Stoichiometry of cocrystal (1:1, 2:1, etc.) is not predicted by HSP alone
Kinetic factors in crystallization (solvent, supersaturation, seeding) are not captured
False negatives are common: some successful cocrystals have large Ra values due to complementary rather than similar interactions
known_failure_modes: Cocrystals stabilized by halogen bonding or pi-pi stacking rather than hydrogen bonding
Systems where crystal packing efficiency dominates over molecular interaction similarity
Zwitterionic APIs where charge-assisted hydrogen bonds override HSP predictions
validation_method: Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) for new phase identification
Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) for melting point and thermal behavior
Single crystal X-ray diffraction for structure determination
Raman or IR spectroscopy for hydrogen bond detection

key_references.author: Mohammad, M.A. et al.
key_references.title: Hansen solubility parameter as a tool to predict cocrystal formation

key_references.author: Abramov, Y.A. et al.
key_references.title: Rational coformer or solvent selection for pharmaceutical cocrystallization or desolvation

key_references.author: Perlovich, G.L.
key_references.title: Thermodynamic characteristics of cocrystal formation and melting points for rational design of pharmaceutical two-component systems

key_references.author: Khalaji, M. et al.
key_references.title: Solubility parameter-based screening for pharmaceutical cocrystal design

literature.regulatory_relevance: FDA draft guidance on pharmaceutical cocrystals (2018) classifies cocrystals as a special drug product; HSP screening is accepted as part of the scientific rationale for coformer selection in regulatory submissions but is not mandated.
